items.myth: A bundler and the TypeScript compiler do the same job.
items.reality: tsc type-checks and can emit JS; a bundler optimizes and packages output and often uses a fast transpiler that doesn't type-check. They're complementary.
items.example: `tsc` = correctness (types). `esbuild`/`swc` (inside Vite) = speed (transpile + bundle). Use both: `tsc --noEmit` for checking, Vite for building.

items.myth: Tree-shaking automatically removes all unused code.
items.reality: It works best with static ESM imports; dynamic requires, side effects, and namespace imports can prevent dead-code elimination.
items.example: A library with `import './setup.css'` or top-level `console.log(...)` has *side effects* — tree-shakers can't safely drop it even if no symbols are used. Mark `"sideEffects": false` in `package.json` for libs that don't have them.

items.reality: Small projects can use native ES modules directly; bundlers earn their keep with many modules, TypeScript, and production optimization needs.
items.example: A 5-file static demo can serve `index.html` + `app.js` via `python -m http.server` with native ESM `<script type='module'>` — no bundler. Add Vite when you want HMR, TS, code splitting, or production minification.
